speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's March the 11th, 1996. The survivor is Alex Davis, born Daviddovits, D-A-V-I-D-O-V-I-T-S. I'm Jacqueline Leone, we are in Hollywood, Florida, USA, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's March the 11th, 1996. The survivor is Alex Davis, D-A-V-O-V-I-T-S. I'm Jacqueline Leone, we are in Hollywood, Florida, USA, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us your name and when and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Alex Davis, D-A-V-I-S and my previous name was Daviddovits, D-A-V-I-D-O-V-I-T-S. I was born in Halmeu, H-A-L-M-E-U, Romania in 1923, October 10th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about what life was like when you were a child in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can begin. We moved from Halmeu, my birth place, to Satumare, which was Romania. It was also called Satumare, S-A-T-U-M-A-R-E. And that's where I grew up. We lived a fairly normal life, although we were a minority. But under Romanian government it was relatively normal. I went to school. It was divided. In the morning I would go to public school. In the afternoon I would go to cheder-- which, later on, I went to higher Jewish education. There was anti-Semitism, but it wasn't condoned. Officially, it wasn't condoned. But from time to time there were outbursts of anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? Outbursts, how?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would physically beat up Jews or they would desecrate certain things. But it was, I would say it was, rather in isolated cases. That part of Romania was Transylvania, which had a-- a large Hungarian population. And there was what you called this cold anti-Semitism. It-- later on in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that manifest itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would call you names, they would shove you off the sidewalks, they would-- those people that had the beards and peyots, you know, would pull them by the beards. Or they would throw stone. As a kid, I remember in public school, we had constant fights and was non-Jews against Jews. And there were always brawlings going on, and they would call you dirty Jew and stinky Jew. And-- and there was discrimination even in the classes, too, to a certain extent. Not manifested openly, but there was always that undercurrent. In the higher school, there was unofficially numerus clausus, which, of course, meant that only certain people could go to college, or to higher education. And this type of anti-Semitism, or feelings, increased with the years. Which-- For instance, I believe it was 1936 that anti-- anti-Semitic government was put into parliament, Romanian parliament. It didn't last too long. It last-- thank God, it didn't last longer-- about six or eight months, and it finally collapsed, and a more democratic government was put into place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come from a large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were [SIGHS] five. At birth, I was a twin but my twin didn't survive. I had four sisters. One emigrated to Israel in 1934, and lived there until her natural life, lived there. And it-- It was a type of life that was more or less similar to the people that conducted in the shtetl-- ultra religious. Religion was a very dominant factor in Jewish life there, and it was shown, it was seen. And like I said, it was-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] there was no open persecution, but there was an undercurrent of anti-Semitism at all times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a teacher. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. He fell ill in approximately in 1930, '31. He-- his vocal cords got ruptured, and he couldn't conduct-- he couldn't do the work anymore. So he became more or less a treasurer for the Suta Mare rabbi that had a [BACKGROUND NOISE] soup kitchen for the Talmudic students. And he was involved for that to collect money for them, and so on. And I, myself, went on to higher learnings in Judaism. I went to three year yeshivot. And up to 1939, I would say between '38 and '39, when I gave up the studies, and I went into to learn business. And I became an apprentice as a textile salesman.

speaker: interviewer
text: You gave up the studies because it was just time to give them up, or because you had to, to help your mother, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I gave it up because I got tired of it, which I deeply regret at this point. Because I was already up in the higher, higher studies. But It was very, very difficult. And naturally, poverty arraigned all around us. And not only that it was a universal depression, but being Jewish, and although there were some very prominent and wealthy Jews in our community, but the vast, vast majority lived in real poverty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the community help each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community help each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Jewish community was very, very active and helped the poor people. To a great extent, they had the-- the Romanian government gave the Jewish leadership, the so-called congress gave them vast powers to tax and to assess members, those that could afford it. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And consequently, they could help out, to some extent, the very poor people, the widows, the orphans, and so on. They had some very, very good organizations, their own hospital, Jewish hospital. They had orphanages. They had [NON-ENGLISH], Hadassahs. It was a well-organized community. This was all very good until 1939. Needless to say, prior to 1939, we had the-- the saber rattlings of the war was constantly in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was going on in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we knew very well. We were-- we, not only through newspapers, we were very, very well-informed of what was going on. [BACKGROUND NOISE] We were very, very knowledgeable about the rising of the Hitler era. As a matter of fact, my father went for medical reasons, went to Berlin in 1931. And he came back in 1932. And I remember, as a-- as a kid, that he told us the story that there is some movement in Germany. And there's this fellow by the name of Hitler that, if he comes to power, then it will be the demise-- it will be the demise of the Jewish people. I remember, it was like a story, you know, that-- that you hear. And then we, of course, we were very knowledgeable about finally what happened with Hitler getting into power and his marching into to-- to Vienna, to Austria, to conquer Austria. First of all, he took the Saarbrucken-- that was his first move that he took away from France, the Saar territory, and which followed by the invasion of Vienna, which later followed the demands of the Sudetenland, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people in Romania feel like it was-- that they were in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people felt very, very much. And now, having said that the greatest majority of the population there were Hungarians, they rooted for the Germans. And they were-- but we were in trepidation. We knew what-- what was following. And so when they marched into Czechoslovakia, and they occupied the Sudetenland, we saw that the noose is gettin' tighter and tighter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] We, of course, we were very well-aware of what happened-- the Sudetenland was decided at Munich. And we knew the episode with Chamberlain, and so on. And we also, which was followed up by Germany demanding the Port of Danzig in Poland. And prior to that, they had occupied-- there is a small port in Lithuania or Latvia-- I don't remember exactly-- which was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they occupied [NON-ENGLISH], which followed by the demand of Danzig. And Poland would not relent. And France and Britain was supporting Poland by not acceding to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your table conversation? I mean, this is what you knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Daily.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a daily basis. Daily, and practically, that occupied our-- out entire life, the conversation of-- of the political, and the situation that was going on and how it's culminating. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Finally, in 19-- [SIGHS] 39, I remember, I had-- I had finished my Judaic studies. In 1939, we saw how the wind is blowing. And on September the 1st, we got to know that Hitler invaded Poland. We were in relative-- in relative security at that time. But we heard what was going on, and we felt very upset-- knowing what was happened to the Jews. But we did not realize-- realize the extent of the danger that was really facing the Jewish people. The first sense that we got about the great danger was already later on in-- in 19-- January, I believe, January, 1940. Prior to that, in September, the 5th of 1940, we were occupied by the Hungarians, part of Transylvania was occupied by the Hungarians. And all of a sudden, overnight, we became Hungarian citizens, so to say. They were already allied with the Germans by that time. And some anti-Jewish laws were coming out slowly. Like Jews, for instance, couldn't go to the market, shopping, till 10 o'clock to give-- to give more time for the non-Jewish population to go to the market first. They wouldn't issue any new-- any licenses for Jewish businesses. And the so-called undercurrent was coming through the surface. And there were anti-Semitic manifestations by more repeated beatings of Jews, and sort of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] physically abusing 'em.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community react to these as a community?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] They probably did, but they didn't go too far. For the Hungarians, you didn't go too far. There were-- was the Romanians-- you could buy your way out many times. You couldn't do that with the Hungarians. And they were also dominated very strongly by the German anti-Jewish policies. But nonetheless, we could still breathe. We could still live, even though they have instituted, by that time, numerus nullus-- in other words, Jews couldn't-- Jews couldn't go to colleges. A Jew couldn't open up a business, unless-- unless he couldn't employ-- a Jewish corporation couldn't employ Jewish people. He had to have 96% non-Jews. Only 6% could-- could be in managerial position-- only 6%. You were relegated to manual work only. But you lived. And of course, the-- the war scene took a stranglehold. Everything was rationed. And Jews did not enjoy the same rationing as non-Jews. But you went around. You worked around it, and you lived. In May of 19-- 1941, I decided to leave my hometown because Halmeu is a smaller town-- not small, it was about 80,000-- 80,000 to 100,000 inhabitants. And I moved up to Budapest. In Budapest--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family, your mother, your father, how did they react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't like it, but they didn't have very much to offer for me in support. By that time, I was already my 18th year, and so I had to fend for myself. And I had already acquired the knowledge of salesmanship in the textile business. So I went up to Budapest for better-- to-- to seek something better. You get lost-- in a bigger city, you get lost. And there, while anti-Semitism existed, and you knew that existed, but you could sort of mingle more. There were great restrictions by that time. They have eliminated all the Jewish art, like actors and actresses couldn't appear, Jewish actresses couldn't appear, and Jewish music wouldn't be played openly. But the Jews organized themselves. And they created their own theaters. As for myself, I-- I couldn't become a salesman, per se, because like I told you, because of the numerus clausus, in this respect-- only 6%. Like for instance, if I want to work in a store, they have to have at least two, three non-Jewish salesmen before I could hold a job. It's random percentages. And that's how life was going on. And we lived modestly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in number 21 [NON-ENGLISH]-- which is-- in English, you would call it Elizabeth Road or Boulevard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an apartment, or did you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a room. No, no, I just rented a room. And I lived on my own. And there, because I have finished Romanian school only, and I only finished some public schools, I tried to excel in Hungarian, and I took the equivalent of four-year high school, night courses. And I also took courses in languages, so I studied English. I studied German, studied-- study Spanish. And I was more into studies during my spare time. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And this is how life was going on until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So between '41 when you got to Budapest and '44, you lived alone--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived a fairly normal--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to school, and you worked? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not open-- well, I belonged to an organization prior, and I-- I-- we'll sort of backtrack a little bit. Their first inclination that we got about the Polish Jews was in January of 1941, when a train of Polish refugees were passing by our city, and on their way to Constanta, to a Romanian port through the Black Sea, a pantheon-- on their way to Palestine, through that time, Palestine. And I was a Betar at that time. I belonged to an organization, Betar, a Zionist organization. And as young kids, we were told that we have to go out to the train to-- to take some food. [CRYING] [DRINKS] And we saw all these Jewish families. [CRYING] And that was the first time we knew what was wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to talk to the people, and they could tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked to the people, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you what--

speaker: interviewee
text: They cried, and they left their homes. And I remembered that we gave-- we gave a piece of chocolate to one man. And he cried. He said that, I owned a chocolate factory. And a year before they came to me, and they asked me for help, and-- and I declined. And now, I take a chocolate from you. And he cried very bitterly. This is, I remember, I was a kid. And so we lived with this start all the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was going on until 1944. I lived in Budapest, and until the beginning of March, March 4th, I believe it was, that all of a sudden the German army swooped down on Budapest on a Sunday afternoon, and they occupied Hungary. Within-- I'll have to backtrack again. And within one week, all the anti-Jewish laws were [BACKGROUND NOISE] handed out with a yellow star to be affixed under the threat of death. And a Jew could not go on a train any more. And even if I wanted to go home, I couldn't go home. And a Jew couldn't go out. There was a certain curfew. You couldn't-- you couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been going back and forth to Romania to visit your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only once, only once did I go back during the time that I left Suta Mare. But prior to that, and like I said, I'll backtrack. In 1942, there was a great influx of Slovak Jewish refugees that because they had the noose, again, tightened up on 'em, and that [NON-ENGLISH], the Nazi Governor took over. And the Jews were fleeing into Hungary. And as it happens, my sister was a little bit older than I am. She was living in Suta Mare, and she was with a committee that was making documents for these Slovak Jews. And she-- they got caught. They got caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister got caught with the whole committee, got caught. And they searched our house in Suta Mare, and they found some correspondence from me in Budapest. So immediately, it was immediately after Rosh Hashanah, I got word from my father that my sister was arrested, and that I ought to go to see whether I could do something in Budapest, which I did. But I was told that she was taken to a place that deals with anti-espionage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Immediately after Rosh Hashanah, I remember the date. There was around 4 o'clock in the morning, there was a knock on my door. And two detectives came in. And you have so and so? And yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started to search my place. They looked through my books. I was studying at that time English, German, Spanish. And they started to throw books at me. Bottom line, they put me into handcuffs. And they arrested me. They took me down to the police station. And they finally took me to a place in-- in shackles, in chains and shackles. And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put through some papers. They interrogated me. And they thought that I was also involved in the documentation. And then, they'll come.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll finish this story--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when we start the next tape.
